lines.text: Section eight. Controlling the flow of information, starting with transcription.
An interesting observation from genome sequencing: complexity does not seem to live in raw gene count.
Humans have around 25,000 protein-coding genes.
E. coli, the well-studied bacterium, has about 4,800.
Five-fold difference for organisms that are obviously not five-fold different in sophistication.
So if complexity is not about how many genes you have, where does it live?
A large part of the answer is regulation.
Higher organisms devote a much larger fraction of their genomes to genes that control other genes — transcription factors, regulatory RNAs, chromatin modifiers, and so on.
The biological work of being a complex organism is largely the work of controlling when, where, and how strongly each gene is expressed.

For an engineer, this is encouraging: it suggests that to build interesting biological behaviour, you do not have to invent new gene products; you can often just build new control logic around existing ones.
Why do cells need that control?
In a eukaryote, gene regulation is how a skin cell ends up different from a brain cell despite having identical DNA — different sets of genes are being expressed in each.
In a bacterium, gene regulation is how the cell responds to its environment — different food sources, different stresses, different temperatures all drive different expression patterns.
And in synthetic biology, gene regulation is the thing you are designing.

Whether you want a strain that produces a chemical only when a sensor detects a signal, or a circuit that toggles between two states, or a programmable response to a stimulus — you are designing transcriptional and translational control.

Several research papers — Pfleger and colleagues in 2006, Pitera and colleagues in 2007 — established early that the fine balance of expression levels in a designed pathway is critical: too much enzyme can be as bad as too little, and tuning the levels is often the difference between a working strain and a failed one.
So we need to look at the actual mechanisms.
We will focus on prokaryotes — bacteria.
Eukaryotic transcription is far more elaborate, with nucleosomes, chromatin remodelling, splicing, and many other layers; the principles are similar but the machinery is heavier.
Most synthetic biology to date has been done in microbial chassis, so the prokaryotic story is the right place to start.
Transcription begins with the enzyme RNA polymerase, which I will sometimes shorten to RNAP.
It is the enzyme that reads a DNA template and produces a complementary mRNA strand.
Just like DNA polymerase, RNAP works in a 5-prime to 3-prime direction on the new RNA strand, which means it reads the template DNA strand 3-prime to 5-prime.
By convention, when biologists draw or sequence a gene, they show the "coding strand", which has the same sequence as the mRNA — with T's standing in for the U's that the actual mRNA would carry.
But it is the other strand, the "template strand", that the polymerase physically reads.
Now, a gene, as we said, is not just the coding sequence — it includes regulatory elements.
Of those, the most important is the promoter.
The promoter is the stretch of DNA, upstream of the coding sequence, where RNA polymerase docks and decides whether to start transcribing.
If you want to think of a gene as having an "on/off switch", the promoter is the switch.
In bacteria, a single promoter can drive a single gene, or it can drive an entire operon — a group of related genes clustered together and co-transcribed from the same promoter.
The classic example is the lac operon in E. coli, where the genes for lactose metabolism are organised in one operon, so the cell can turn on lactose digestion as a single coordinated decision.
Operons are essentially the bacterial idiom for grouping a set of functionally related genes under one regulatory decision.
Now, bacterial RNA polymerase has a quirk: by itself, it cannot find or bind a promoter.
It needs a helper protein called a sigma factor, written with the Greek letter sigma.
The sigma factor is what recognises the specific DNA sequences in the promoter region and guides RNA polymerase to bind there.
When RNA polymerase and a sigma factor are combined, the resulting active complex is called the holoenzyme.
E. coli has seven different sigma factors, each recognising a different family of promoters, and the cell uses them to coordinate large-scale gene regulation in response to different conditions.
Let me name them.
Sigma-70, also called RpoD, is the housekeeping sigma factor — it directs the polymerase to most genes that are needed under normal growth conditions.
Sigma-54, RpoN, handles nitrogen limitation.
Sigma-38, RpoS, handles stationary phase and starvation.
Sigma-32, RpoH, handles heat shock — it is the one whose level rises when the cell gets too hot.
Sigma-28, RpoF, handles flagellar gene expression.
Sigma-24, RpoE, handles extreme stress, particularly trouble at the cell envelope.
And sigma-19, FecI, handles ferric citrate transport — the iron uptake machinery.
By regulating the abundance and activity of each sigma factor, the cell can swap which whole programme of genes is being transcribed.
It is a tidy mechanism — turning on dozens of related genes via the abundance of one sigma factor — and you will see synthetic biologists co-opt it.
Where on the DNA does the sigma factor actually look?
The transcription start site — the first base that gets copied into mRNA — is designated the plus-one position.
Two key promoter elements sit upstream of plus-one.
There is a conserved sequence around the minus-ten position, and another around the minus-thirty-five position.
These are the recognition sites that sigma factors read.
The specific consensus sequence at minus-ten and minus-thirty-five differs slightly between sigma factors, which is what makes one sigma activate a different set of promoters than another.
And to make this even more layered, there are anti-sigma factors that bind to sigma factors and inhibit them, and anti-anti-sigma factors that bind to anti-sigma factors and restore activity.
It is regulatory turtles, several deep.
Sigma factors are not the only way to control transcription.
Two other classes of proteins are critical: activators and repressors.
An activator binds at or near a specific promoter and increases the rate of transcription from it.
A repressor binds at or near a specific promoter and decreases the rate of transcription from it.

Both activators and repressors are called transcription factors, and unlike the global, genome-wide effect of sigma factors, transcription factors typically act on a single gene or a small set of related genes.
Now one final point about how transcription factors give you a sharp on-off switch.
If a single repressor molecule binds a single site on DNA, the relationship between repressor concentration and gene expression is a hyperbolic curve — gradual, gentle, hard to fully shut off.
But many transcription factors bind cooperatively, often as dimers or tetramers, and to multiple binding sites in the operator region.
When the first repressor binds, it makes it easier for the second to bind too — protein-protein contacts plus protein-DNA contacts — and you end up with a sigmoidal, S-shaped response curve.
Sigmoidal means sharp.
A sigmoidal response curve gives you something close to a digital switch: below a threshold the gene is essentially on, above it the gene is essentially off, and the transition is fast.
This is why so many synthetic biology circuits depend on cooperatively binding repressors — the sharper the switch, the cleaner the circuit behaves.

Mark Ptashne's classic book on the bacteriophage lambda repressor, published in 1992, is the canonical reference if you want to dig deeper into how cooperativity gives biology digital-feeling switches; the textbook explicitly recommends it.
Modelling these interactions, even with simple Hill-type equations, will teach you a lot about how much engineering leverage you can get by tweaking binding affinities and cooperativity.
